title.title: Build & Integration
title.subtitle: Standards, frameworks, platforms, and integration patterns for taking smart contracts to production

erc-20.purpose: Cryptocurrencies, DeFi tokens, governance tokens, stablecoins.
erc-20.takeaway: The "standard money" of Ethereum — every wallet and exchange supports it.

erc-721.purpose: Unique digital items: art, collectibles, tickets, in-game assets, real-estate deeds.
erc-721.takeaway: Enables NFTs — one-of-a-kind, ownable digital assets.

erc-777.purpose: Complex DeFi apps and tokens needing smart-contract receive hooks.
erc-777.takeaway: Smarter version of ERC-20 — more flexible for contract-to-contract use. Adoption limited; ERC-20 dominates.

erc-2981.purpose: Provide royalty payout info to NFT marketplaces in a standardized way.
erc-2981.takeaway: Standard way to support artist and creator royalties on resales — fixes a gap in ERC-721.

erc-1155.purpose: Both fungible and non-fungible tokens in a single contract; ideal for gaming and batch transfers.
erc-1155.takeaway: Efficient and flexible — batch transfers cut gas costs by 80–90%. Common in gaming/NFTs.

1.label: Community discussion
1.desc: Idea proposed informally on forums, social media, conferences.

2.label: Ethereum Magicians
2.desc: Informal feedback from technical contributors. Discussion thread refines the proposal.

3.label: Draft EIP on GitHub
3.desc: PR submitted to ethereum/EIPs repository in standardized format.

4.label: EIP Editors review format
4.desc: Editors review for completeness and adherence to template — not technical merit.

5.label: Repository assigns number
5.desc: EIP assigned a unique number and merged in Draft status.

6.label: Public discussion
6.desc: Wider technical and economic review. Proposal evolves through revisions.

7.label: Client teams review feasibility
7.desc: Geth, Nethermind, Besu, Reth, Erigon teams assess implementation feasibility.

8.label: AllCoreDevs decision
8.desc: For protocol-level EIPs: rough consensus among core developers across clients.

9.label: EIP Status: Final / Accepted
9.desc: Status updated to Final. Specification frozen — no further edits.

10.label: Clients implement
10.desc: Each Ethereum client implements the EIP in code, tests across testnets.

11.label: Network activates (fork)
11.desc: Hard fork at scheduled block height activates the change for everyone.

hardhat.cons: Can be overkill for small projects

eth.desc: Global consensus (PoS). Most mature ecosystem; high gas, broad tooling.

fabric.desc: Pluggable consensus, channel-based privacy. No gas. Enterprise-grade.

sol.desc: PoH + PoS. High throughput. Rust required; Sealevel parallel runtime.

cardano.desc: UTxO-based, Ouroboros PoS. Formal verification focus.

bnb.desc: EVM-compatible fork. Fast, low-cost, broad tool support.

avax.desc: EVM-compatible subnets supported. Custom chains possible.

arb.desc: Lower fees, uses Ethereum L1 for security. Mature L2.

op.desc: Near-identical to Ethereum dev flow. OP Stack used by Base.

base.desc: Coinbase-hosted L2. Same stack as Optimism, friendly UX.

starknet.desc: ZK-rollup with custom VM. Cairo language. Growing ecosystem.

dot.desc: Modular setup, high customisation across parachains.

algo.desc: Layer-1 logic. Fast finality, pure PoS.

corda.desc: Finance-oriented. State machines, no global gossip.

ercExplorer.heading: ERC Standards Explorer
ercExplorer.subtitle: Click any ERC to see its functions, events, and where it's used in the wild.

eipFlow.heading: How an EIP becomes a standard
eipFlow.subtitle: From idea to network upgrade — click any step to see what happens.

fwCompare.heading: Smart Contract Frameworks · Side by Side
fwCompare.subtitle: Click a row label to highlight that comparison dimension across all frameworks.

checklist.heading: Platform Selection Checklist
checklist.subtitle: Toggle the requirements that matter for your use case. The top 5 matching platforms surface live.
checklist.empty: Toggle some requirements on the left to see ranked recommendations.

items.evm: Need EVM compatibility (broad tooling, audited contracts)?

standardsIntro.heading: Smart Contract Standards
standardsIntro.subtitle: Formalised guidelines that make smart contracts interoperable, secure, and predictable across platforms.
standardsIntro.whyBody: they enable interoperability with wallets, exchanges (CEX/DEX), DeFi protocols, marketplaces, and other dApps — by ensuring consistent behaviors and interfaces. Inheriting from a standard means your contract works "out of the box" with the entire ecosystem.

ercExplain.heading: What is an ERC?
ercExplain.subtitle: "Ethereum Request for Comments" — community-driven proposals that become standards.
ercExplain.definitionBody1B: is a set of proposals from the Ethereum community suggesting new features and improvements to the Ethereum network. Each ERC defines specific functions, interfaces, and behaviors enabling compatibility across wallets, exchanges, and other Ethereum-based tools.
ercExplain.definitionBody2: The naming follows other open standards bodies — IETF RFCs, Python PEPs, Bitcoin BIPs. Ethereum took inspiration from all of them to create the EIP/ERC process.
eipsVsErcs: = Ethereum Improvement Proposal — covers protocol-level, networking, ERC, and informational changes
= a sub-category of EIPs specifically for application-level standards (tokens, contracts, conventions)
All ERCs are EIPs, but not all EIPs are ERCs
Both follow the same standardization process

ercEvolve.heading: Why Standards Evolve
ercEvolve.subtitle: New use cases emerge; old standards expose limitations; community innovation drives proposals forward.

cards.title: New use cases
cards.desc: DAOs, decentralized identity, gaming, DePIN — each expansion of the ecosystem reveals patterns that benefit from formal standardization.

cards.title: Limitations exposed
cards.desc: ERC-721 had no royalty mechanism — leading to ERC-2981. Marketplaces ignored royalties → led to operator filters → led to renewed efforts.

cards.title: Community innovation
cards.desc: Anyone can submit an EIP. Community discussion through Magicians forum, GitHub, AllCoreDevs calls drives proposals to maturity.

ercEvolve.exampleBody: ERC-2981 (NFT royalty standard) was created because ERC-721 had no built-in mechanism for royalty enforcement. Artists were not getting paid on secondary sales. ERC-2981 standardized the royaltyInfo function so marketplaces could query and respect royalties automatically. The standard process took ~2 years from proposal to widespread adoption.

ercExample.heading: ERC-20 in Solidity · OpenZeppelin
ercExample.subtitle: The audited, battle-tested way to deploy a fungible token.

points.label: Importing
points.desc: import "@openzeppelin/contracts/…/ERC20.sol"; brings in all the audited functions, events, and behaviors of the ERC-20 standard.

points.label: Inheriting
points.desc: contract MyToken is ERC20 — your contract follows the standard interface. Wallets, DEXs, block explorers all recognise it without configuration.

points.label: Extending
points.desc: You can add custom logic on top — burn(), pause(), mint controls, transfer fees — by inheriting more OpenZeppelin modules.

points.label: Compatibility
points.desc: Because you inherit ERC-20 from OpenZeppelin, your token works out-of-the-box with wallets, DEXs, and indexers — no integrations to write.

ercExample.whyOzBody: The contracts are audited, battle-tested in billions of dollars of value. Writing your own ERC-20 from scratch is a security risk you almost never need to take.

frameworksIntro.heading: Smart Contract Frameworks
frameworksIntro.subtitle: Developer toolkits for the entire contract lifecycle — write, compile, test, deploy, upgrade.

lifecycle.label: Project scaffolding
lifecycle.desc: Folder structure, config files, sensible defaults

lifecycle.label: Compilation
lifecycle.desc: Compile Solidity → bytecode + ABI; manage compiler versions

lifecycle.label: Local blockchain simulation
lifecycle.desc: Spin up an in-memory chain with funded test accounts

lifecycle.label: Scriptable deployments
lifecycle.desc: Reproducible deploys to testnets and mainnets

lifecycle.label: Testing frameworks
lifecycle.desc: Unit and integration tests with assertions and fixtures

production.label: Gas analysis
production.desc: Per-function gas usage; optimise hot paths before deployment

production.label: Contract verification
production.desc: Submit source to Etherscan/Sourcify — increases user trust

production.label: Frontend integration
production.desc: ABI generation, TypeScript types, wagmi/ethers helpers

production.label: Plugin ecosystem
production.desc: Extend with linting, deployment helpers, security scanners

production.label: Upgradeable contracts
production.desc: Proxy patterns (UUPS, Transparent) for live upgrades

tools.heading: Useful Tools
tools.subtitle: Web-based playgrounds and learning resources every smart contract developer should know.

items.name: Remix IDE
items.desc: Browser-based Solidity IDE — write, compile, deploy, debug, all in your browser. Ideal for prototyping.

items.name: Eth.build
items.desc: Visual sandbox for understanding Ethereum mechanics — drag-and-drop building of contract interactions.

items.name: SpeedRun Ethereum
items.desc: Curriculum of progressively harder challenges — staker, vendor, dice game, multi-sig — gives you portfolio projects.

items.name: Scaffold-ETH
items.desc: Open-source toolkit for building dApps on Ethereum. Comes with Hardhat, Next.js, ethers, debug UI — full stack.

platformUsecase.heading: Use Case → Recommended Platforms
platformUsecase.subtitle: Quick-reference mapping from common use cases to platforms that fit them.

items.platforms: Ethereum · Arbitrum · Optimism · Polygon · BNB Chain · zkSync
Polygon · BNB Chain · Avalanche · zkSync · Base
Solana · Near · Polygon · Arbitrum · Avalanche subnets

integrationIntro.heading: Integration Best Practices
integrationIntro.subtitle: Connecting smart contracts to existing systems without breaking either side.
integrationIntro.lead: Seamless integration is essential — existing software systems should keep operating efficiently while harnessing the benefits of automation, transparency, and trust. Five integration patterns dominate production deployments:

nodes.heading: Node Infrastructure
nodes.subtitle: Every interaction with a smart contract passes through a node. Choose carefully.
nodes.providersValue: Infura · Alchemy · QuickNode · Ankr · Blast API · Tenderly.

api.heading: API & Middleware
api.subtitle: Bridges between smart contracts and legacy systems (ERPs, CRMs, databases).

does.label: Trigger contract functions
does.desc: Off-chain backend calls a contract method without manual blockchain knowledge

does.label: Pull data for dashboards
does.desc: Index events and state for analytics, reporting, user-facing UIs

does.label: Abstract complexity
does.desc: Wallet connection, gas estimation, RPC retries — handled by the library

stacks.name: Ethers.js / viem
stacks.desc: Direct contract interaction libraries — closest to the metal. Most production dApps use these.

stacks.name: The Graph
stacks.desc: Decentralized indexer — write a "subgraph" that turns on-chain events into a queryable GraphQL API.

stacks.name: Moralis
stacks.desc: Web3-as-a-service: indexed data, cross-chain APIs, auth. Faster to start, but more vendor lock-in.

stacks.name: Alchemy SDK / Infura SDK
stacks.desc: Provider-specific SDKs with batching, websockets, and enriched APIs (NFT metadata, transaction history).

events.heading: Event-Driven Architecture
events.subtitle: Smart contracts emit events. Off-chain services listen and act on them — like webhooks for the blockchain.
flowSteps: Smart contract executes a function and emits an event (e.g.
Off-chain service subscribes to that event via Web3 WebSocket / The Graph / Moralis
On event arrival, the service triggers downstream actions: update CRM, send email, generate document, update dashboard
State stays in sync between blockchain and traditional systems

examples.label: Supply chain:
examples.text: Delivery confirmed event → ERP marks invoice as paid → notify finance

examples.label: DeFi:
examples.text: Liquidity added event → analytics dashboard updates → user gets push notification

examples.label: Compliance:
examples.text: Large transfer event → AML system creates suspicious activity report

examples.label: Gaming:
examples.text: Item minted event → Discord bot announces in player guild channel

events.whyBody: real-time responsiveness for dynamic workflows like supply chain updates, DeFi flows, or compliance alerts.

oracles.heading: Orchestration with Oracles
oracles.subtitle: Smart contracts can't natively access real-world data. Oracles are the bridges.

do.label: Pull data:
do.text: weather, stock prices, sports scores, ID verification, IoT sensor readings

do.label: Push events:
do.text: trigger an IoT actuator, notify a user via Telegram, send a text message

do.label: Compute off-chain:
do.text: run heavy computation, submit only the result on-chain

oracles.criticalBody: a single oracle = a single point of failure. Use decentralized oracle networks for any value-bearing contract.

providers.name: Chainlink
providers.desc: Industry standard. Decentralized network, broad data feeds, strong security track record.

providers.name: Pyth Network
providers.desc: High-frequency financial data — direct from exchanges and market makers. ~400ms latency.

providers.name: API3
providers.desc: First-party oracles — data providers run their own nodes. No middleman.

providers.name: UMA Optimistic
providers.desc: Assume data correct unless disputed within a window. Cheaper, slower for edge cases.

data.heading: Data Management · On-chain vs Off-chain
data.subtitle: Blockchain is not designed for large-scale, mutable, or sensitive data. Be deliberate.
onchain: Anything that NEEDS the trust guarantees of the chain
offchain: Documents, images, video — too expensive on-chain
PII (names, emails, health data) — privacy / GDPR risk
Bulk historical data (move to archive nodes / data lakes)
Anything you might need to delete (right to be forgotten)

storage.name: IPFS
storage.desc: Content-addressed; pin via Pinata or Filebase

storage.name: Filecoin
storage.desc: IPFS + paid persistence with crypto-economic guarantees

aiDev.heading: AI-Driven Smart Contract Development
aiDev.subtitle: Use AI tools where they shine — and know where they don't belong.

helps.label: Prototyping:
helps.text: Remix + ChatGPT for quick PoCs and learning

helps.label: Coding agents
helps.text: (Codex, Claude Code) for actual implementation work — much better than chat

aiDev.helpsFooter: Use AI as a faster way to learn, prototype, and document. Treat its output as a draft.

not.label: Security-critical logic
not.text: — AI hallucinates plausible-looking but vulnerable code

not.label: Trusting outputs blindly
not.text: — verify every line, especially around access control and arithmetic

not.label: Replacing audits
not.text: — AI cannot reliably catch reentrancy, oracle manipulation, MEV, or flash-loan attacks

aiDev.bottomBody: AI accelerates prototyping and learning. Production contracts still require human expertise, peer review, and professional audit.

takeaways.title: Section 06 — Key Takeaways
items: Standards (ERC-20/721/777/1155/2981) make contracts interoperable with the entire wallet/exchange/marketplace ecosystem
EIP process is open: anyone can propose; community + clients + AllCoreDevs decide. From idea to network upgrade can take months to years
Use OpenZeppelin contracts as your base — audited, battle-tested, save you from rolling your own ERC-20
Hardhat / Foundry are the dominant frameworks for EVM. Foundry is fastest; Hardhat is most extensible. Brownie/Truffle are legacy. Anchor for Solana
Free tools to know: remix.ethereum.org · eth.build · speedrunethereum.com · scaffoldeth.io
Platform selection is multi-dimensional: EVM compatibility · public vs permissioned · throughput · cost · ecosystem · regulatory fit
Integration patterns: nodes (own/managed/hybrid) · APIs (Ethers, viem) · events (subscribe + react) · oracles (Chainlink/Pyth) · on/off-chain split
AI helps with ideation, prototyping, documentation. AI does NOT replace audits, security review, or expert engineering for production code

quiz.question: Your team is building a tokenized real-estate platform. You need: (a) full ERC-20 and ERC-721 support, (b) low gas fees for frequent rent distributions, (c) the ability to deploy proven smart contracts (OpenZeppelin), (d) a regulated EU market presence. Which platform stack fits BEST?
options: Hyperledger Fabric — permissioned, enterprise-grade, no gas fees.
Solana — high throughput, low cost, fast finality.
Polygon (or Arbitrum / Base) — EVM-compatible L2 with full ERC support, low gas fees, OpenZeppelin compatibility, and EU MiCA framework awareness.
Cardano — formal verification focus, Plutus/Marlowe for finance.
quiz.explanation: The use case has 4 specific constraints: full ERC support (rules out non-EVM Solana, Cardano, Hyperledger Fabric), low gas (rules out Ethereum L1 mainnet for frequent distributions), OpenZeppelin compatibility (favors EVM), and EU regulatory fit (favors public chains with MiCA-compliant pathways). Polygon, Arbitrum, and Base all check every box. Hyperledger Fabric is great for enterprise but has zero ERC support and would force you to rebuild every standard. Solana is fast but requires Rust + custom token standards. Cardano's tooling is less mature for tokenized real-estate. The answer is an EVM-compatible L2.
